line_preview: Quick Sample Size Report for Armon et al. (2013)

This is the power analysis used to provide a rough estimate of the sample sized required for a replication attempt. It is a result of a brief review (< 20 minutes) of the paper and uses information that is readily available.
The following is the brief description of the statistical analysis for the focal inferential test in SCORE and the results of that test. See the paper itself for additional background.

Ordinary least squares (OLS) regression was used to test the relationship between big five personality factors on body mass index (BMI), controlling for participant gender, age, years of education, and chronic disease.
Neuroticism (N) was positively associated with BMI at baseline (T1) (beta = .05, B = 0.15, p < 0.05).

For all papers in SCORE, there are two power calculations used to derive the sample sizes that will be used for RR attempts. The first is 90% power, assuming that the true effect size is 75% of that reported in the original study. The second is 90% power, assuming that the true effect size is 50% of that reported in the original study.
For this paper, these calculations were conducted the method shown below:
### Sample size calcuations are conducted here. The source (e.g. Table 2) of each piece of information used is documented in the comments]
Residualdf <- quick_orig_samp_size - 10 # sample size minus 9 freely estimated parmaters + intercept
# reported in Table 3 for the BMI outcome at T1
# unstandardized regression coefficient minus standard error (reported in Table 3 (T1:BMI))
#This generates a range of possible values for the se and point estimate (rounding error).
## Calculate range of possible sample size requirements
#find min and max - exclude all values that are greater than the p < .01 thresholod (original p-value was reported as < .05).
### rang of possible required sample sizes
#identify the record associated with the larges required ss.
# IMPORTANT NOTE: The effect size for multiple regression is Cohen's f-squared.
quick_ss_Stage1 <- ceiling(pwr.f2.test(u = 1, v = NULL, f2 = quick_effect_size_numeric * .75, sig.level = .05, power = .9)$v) + n_parms
quick_ss_Stage2 <- ceiling(pwr.f2.test(u = 1, v = NULL, f2 = quick_effect_size_numeric * .5, sig.level = .05, power = .9)$v) + n_parms
For these calculations, the primary unit of analysis are participants. A preliminary estimate for the required sample size is
a sample of 9625 for stage 1 data collection
